column1: 2024-05-07 - RNZ - The New Yorker Kyle Chayka on
column4: Risk 3.2 is about the loss of consensus on reality, and this is instead about the reinforcement of a homogenous reality through algorithmic sharing. Therefore, this ISN'T risk 3.2, despite how 'filterworld' sounds like a concept which would drive people towards individually-filtered bubbles of content. QUOTE: [The phrase "filterworld" describes the immersive environment of algorithms that are adjusted to what we might like to consume. "An algorithm is just an equation ... and what they judge by is really engagement. So it's what's already popular, what are other people looking at, what are other people clicking on and I think that tends to reinforce this sort of homogeneity. "It funnels us towards the same styles or aesthetics or sounds that work for the biggest number of people."]. IN addition, this article references Risk 6.1. Many of the references to 6.1 are to "what works best for digital platforms" or "what works best for the biggest number of people" rather than directly calling out the concentration of power. While it's talking about reinforcement of a homogenous reality, it isn't about use of AI to create homogenous art (as described in Risk 6.3), but instead it's about reinforcement of paradigms or art that suit the style or heuristics encoded into the AI from the designers (Risk 6.1)
column7: Book author describes the increasing homogenization of real-world spaces as well as online content, which they suspect is due to people outsourcing decision-making to algorithms.
column9: This sits at the interface of Risks 3.2, 5.2, 6.1, and 6.3. However, it doesn’t neatly fit into any domain other than 5.2: it isn't about a loss of consensus on reality; preferences shift towards palatable, rather than reinforcing a hierarchy (though this is quite a strong argument for 6.1); and it isn't about AI being used to generate physical environments, but AI is rather rewarding a particular type of aesthetic via recommender algorithms.

column1: 2024-05-08 - NZH - Kiwis driving start-up gets $1
column4:
There is some explanation of transparency of AI systems, presented as a development that's required to win over regulators. I've classed this as Greater utility and utilization of data as it's producing new data from a dataset (producing explanations of why an AI model made a certain decision). The benefit is presented as producing that data, rather than producing insight from the data (i.e. they aren't studying the phenomenon of self-driving car decision-making; they're attempting to create a dataset that can be scrutinized by regulators if an accident occurs). 
The meaning behind "machines that are in the physical world that we can trust," is unclear, While it's partially explained by the following paragraph, it could be referring to explainability, reliability, or something else entirely.
column5:
Doesn't explain exact safety concerns, so just a Domain 7 risk. Risk 6.5 (that the industry will move too fast for proper regulation) is presented as not an issue, as there has been "intense regulatory scrutiny" and GM "removed its driverless cars from the road last year amid safety and legal concerns."
References to transparency also present these issues as not an issue, based on expected developments ("building software to explain in English why a car made a certain driving decision") and it doesn't represent this as a current issue (i.e. there's no "Currently, it can be unclear why a driverless car made a certain driving decision"). 
Notable that it doesn't mention the increased safety of self-driving cars compared to human drivers.
column7: Profile of an Auckland University engineering grad's company, Wayve, explaining their driverless car technology.
column9:
"With reporting by the New York Times", so may be imported? Unclear whether it's imported or just some sentences taken from NYT content. 
As it has a kiwi journalist as a by-line, I'll keep in the sample. Additionally, while some of the article is about the founder, there is a lot of detailed focus on the technology that isn't about the financials, so I think it's in scope.

column1: 2024-05-19 - PRS - No need to scan-       Superma
column3:
The benefits included near the end are implicit (updatability and interoperability): There's a benefit of using AI for this application because it can be updated via specifying the fruits that it wasn't previously trained on in China; there's also interoperability enabling them to use different hardware for the same AI system (the accredited scales). However, this is a relatively clunky example of interoperability... as they needed to ship them back to China. 
The final sentence references three benefits: Faster checkout (higher productivity for business), more informed decision-making (about what stock to order), and greater reliability in their service (by ensuring they have products on the shelf, resulting in fewer shoppers going home empty-handed).
column4: There's a sentence about how it cannot yet weigh two different produce items at once; only one type of produce can be weighed and priced. While this is a comment about the lack of capability, it isn't talking about a risk of it being incapable for normal use - just a limitation on the current technology that has resulted in rules of intended use (only weigh one fruit at a time). Therefore, this comment is NOT classed as Risk 7.3 (lack of capabilities for intended use).
column7: A new AI camera supports faster weighing of fruits and vegetables at the checkout counter. They also mention automated shelf-scanning.

column1: 2024-05-19 - RNZ - Police use images from number
column4:
This article seems to imply misuse by police in the current day, and refrences misuse two years ago. Therefore, I have counted this as a referenced to AI-assisted surveillance or abuse of power (Risk 4.1).
It also states that thousands of officers have authorized access to Auror and SaferCities, but doesn't indicate that this is a privacy risk. Therefore, I haven't counted this as a reference to Risk 2 - privacy and security issues.
It also gives an example of the current policy, implying that Risk 6.5 (governance unable to appropriately regulate with new technology) is not a valid concern. It doesn't directly state a risk of governance being unable to regulate new technology, so I have not counted it as a governance risk.
column7: Describes current police use of ANPR and context relevant to an ongoing legal challenge.
column9: As the article demonstrates that there's an ongoing investigation into police misuse, and also describes a current policy that ensures apporpriate use of ANPR, as well as some reasons why ANPR may be useful for improving Police's productivity. Therefore, this article presents both risks and benefits, as well as reasons why the stated risk may not actually be a concern.

column1: 2024-05-27 - RNZ - New Zealand continues to call
column3: I am interpreting the reference to interoperability as saying: "we need to ensure that we can buy or use technology that enables us to defend ourselves (national security), and maintain ability to work with security partners' technology (interoperability)." This doesn't necessarily refer to AI being better than other tech because it's interoperable with our allies' technology. It *might* be stating that AI tech is important as it maintains interoperability as allies get smarter tech, but it might also be referring to non-AI technology that can interface with AI technology (and indeed, security partners updating to systems that *aren't* interoperable with non-AI systems would be a counter-example to the statement "A benefit of AI is that it is highly interoperable with other systems.") Because I can't exactly confirm what the meaning of this sentence is, I will NOT record it as an example of "Benefit - Interoperability."
column4: Provides a lot of commentary on the difficulties of advancing policy and governance of AI weapons systems, and does explicitly reference the necessity of human oversight, and consequences of AI use that doesn't have explicit oversight. Therefore, this does count as a reference to risk 6.5.

column9: Some of the examples aren't clearly current systems. E.g. "responsible militaries will develop and use AI-enabled capabilities in innovative ways to strengthen protections" refers to tech that isn't yet developed.

column1: 2024-07-08 - SPN - Fake news, AI slop and little
column4: This article uses a lot of irony to describe bad things happening with AI (Meta's content recommender algorithm, and AI slop). There aren't many explicit statements about the rangers or harms, but the tone is very clear that these are harmful or negative, and therefore I'm counting these as references to the risk (where in some other articles, description of a harm occuring without explicitly referencing the risk has been considered not a reference). Examples from this article: Describes a number of alarming or disturbing images (Risk 1.2, toxic content). Across all the references to scams, it seems like it's clearly talking about AI-generated images used to scam people (Risk 4.3). Talks about perception that media must adopt AI to keep up (Risk 6.2), otherwise they'll lose their ability to do business. Describes lack of regulation being unable to be cleaned up (Risk 6.5). While the final 5 paragraphs seems to be talking about loss of consensus on reality, they emphasize the wasteland feeling rather than the fractered perceptions of reality, so I haven't counted this as a reference to Risk 3.2, but rather a reference to the increasing distance between humans, and increased disconnection. Additionally, references to misinformation are generally references to deliberately-fake content (Risk 4.3, scams), rather than indicental production of misinformation (Risk 3.1). Alternatively, other references are to misinformation that aren't clearly AI generated - it could just be recommended content that was produced by a human on the platform.

Part way down page, Shastry raises concern that the AI bot is not capable at the task that it's intended for QUOTE [Shastru told Stuff he didn't trust the bots.] and that there's a lack of transparency (Risks 7.3 and 7.4 respectively). Throughout the article, references to how impersonal it is (A risk I've called out separately from the MIT domain taxonomy) and additionally, applicants feel a loss of agency in having so many decisions about their lives made by an algorithm (Risk 5.2). THere are references to discrimination that are clearly Risk 1.1 (AI learning incorrect patterns from the data, such as learning that people who don't have an English first name aren't likely to be highly-ranked candidates), as well as references to discrimination that are less clear (thus Domain 1 without a subdomain), such as discrimination based on different words and phrases, or tone, which seems more likely to be Risk 1.3 (unequal performance across groups) but it isn't clear from the information provided.
column5: There's a reference to "no facial screning/recognition being used at any point", which seems to be intented to suggest that there isn't risk of abuse of facial recognition. However, it's unclear exactly which type of risk they're avoiding or mitigating, here. It could be mitigating risk of data being recorded or stolen (Privacy and security risks), of facial recognition being used to envigilate job assessments (possibly biased, RIsk 1.3, or possibly degradation of working conditions Risk 6.2). Therefore, I've coded this as "Risk negated - not enough information to classify domain).

column1: 2024-10-19 - RNZ - Why Google Maps cant pronounce
column4: Article describes lower performance in te reo Māori compared to other languages, indicating a lack of capability in a particular subgroup (as it is trained on some Māori data, but likely not as much as other langauges). This also refers to Risk 6.1 (power concentration, and reinforcement of cultural norms that reinforce those who have AI-based power), as the article states that the benchmark is lower for te reo Māori, and the statement that no Māori-langauge speakers were involved in the creation of tools like OpenAI and Google's AI voice models.
column7: International AI voice tools, like OpenAI and Google's tools, are not as accurate as Te Hiku Media's te reo Māori pronounciation AI tool.

column1: 2025-04-09 - BOP - MSD denies plans for automated
column4: This article is difficult to code risks for. The obvious ones: Risk 1 (bias in decision-making) because they're talking about the necessity of designing a system consistent with the standard so that everyone gets their correct entitlement and isnt' discriminated against. They're also talking about the potential risk of increasing hardship for vulnerable populations. While this COULD refer to loss of agency, it doesn't explicitly say this (which would be Risk 5.2). I think they're referring to Risk 6.1 (that the use of AI benefits the company using AI, but not MSD's beneficiaries).
column7: Salvation Army and Law Society react to the government's law change, out of fear that it will introduce the use of AI to benefits.
column9: The article is largely talking about a hypothetical introduction of automated decision-making that isn't planned, but just is suspected based on a proposed law change.

column1: 2025-04-09 - RNZ - Ministry of Social Development
column4: Unclear exactly which type of risk this is referencing, for Bias and discrimination, so I'll code as Risk 1 rather than a specific subdomain. This article talks about the lack of capability of the AI system to account for the complexities of the decisions MSD makes (Risk 7.3, lack of capability at the task it's designed for). Similar to above, it talks about the use of automated systems which may benefit the company deploying it, but not the beneficiaries of MSD (Risk 6.1).

column1: 2025-04-09 - RNZ - There should always be a human
column4: This article referneces the risk of AI being used to help develop weapons (Risk 4.2), as well as AI being used alongside or to control lethal weapons (Risk 7.2). Additionally, there's a lot of reference to the challenge of governing AI internationally. Finally, there's a mention of using drones for surveillance, but this isn't clear they're talking about AI specifically.
column5: Doesn't reference risk 5.2 (loss of human agency and autonomy), as it doesn't mention the loss of military ability to control the killer drone-related actions. Instead, it refers to Risk 7.2 (AI posessing dangerous capabilities) where an AI may fail without adequate safeguards in place. The inclusion of a human in the loop is an example of a safeguard. Similarly, mentioned that NZ was acquiring drones to protect us agianst other drones, and also the increase in military budgets as % of GDP in US and Europe (i.e. an example of an arms race) but doesn't paint this as a risk or potential for the arms race to make the nature of warfare more dangerous, and therefore this doesn't qualify as a reference to Risk 6.4.
column7: Describes New Zealand and international approaches to use of autonomous drones in warfare and defense.

column1: 2025-04-14 - RNZ - AI widely used in public secto
column4: "Worried about the use of AI in recruitment and promotion decisions" does seem to me to be a reference to relying on the use of AI without applying a person's critical thinking in the processes (Risk 5.1) This is combined with Risk 1.1 of discrimination in these applications, so I'll code it as a reference to both. Additionally, it seems reasonable to assume that they're worried about Risk 1.1 (discrimination through learned bias) becuase they're talking about decision-making systems here.
column7: Describes results of a recent survey about public and community services' use of AI.
column9: Although there's a reference to a benefit, the overall tone is negative so I've coded this as negative towards AI.

column1: 2025-04-16 - NAD - AI readiness slips despite inv
column4: Doesn't explicitly mention the harms that may come from a race to deploy AI systems, and doesn't seem to be referencing the AI race as a potential risk (instead, just seems to be mentioning it to build undersanding of how people are currently acting). Therefore, I haven't coded this as Risk 6.4. There's a risk that expected returns from AI fail to eventuate, which doesn't fit under any specific risk in the domain taxonomy. Finally, the following reference was vague enough that it could refer to any socioeconomic risk, so I coded this as Risk Domain 6 without a subdomain: "â€œAt the same time, it is crucial that all stakeholders ... work together to develop local talent so that the entire ecosystem can benefit.â€"
column7: AI readiness index results came back, lower than expected
